questions.question: Explain the fundamental difference between a half-adder and a full-adder circuit. Why is the half-adder insufficient for building a multi-bit adder?
keyPoints: A half-adder adds two single bits and produces a sum and a carry-out.
A full-adder adds three single bits (two inputs plus a carry-in) and produces a sum and a carry-out.
The half-adder has no input to account for a carry from a previous, less significant bit addition.
Therefore, a half-adder cannot be used to construct a ripple-carry adder for multi-bit numbers, whereas full-adders can be chained together using their carry-in and carry-out ports.

questions.question: Describe the purpose and function of a ripple-carry adder (RCA). What is its primary limitation, and how does the carry-lookahead adder (CLA) attempt to overcome this?
keyPoints: A ripple-carry adder is built by chaining together full-adders to add two multi-bit binary numbers.
The carry-out from one stage becomes the carry-in to the next, causing the carry to 'ripple' through the circuit from the LSB to the MSB.
The primary limitation is its speed, as the worst-case delay is proportional to the number of bits (n), due to the carry propagation delay.
A carry-lookahead adder (CLA) uses a separate circuit to generate carry signals in parallel, based on 'generate' (G) and 'propagate' (P) signals.
This reduces the carry delay from being linear (O(n)) to being logarithmic (O(log n)), significantly speeding up the addition operation for large n.

questions.question: Explain, with the aid of a logic circuit diagram, how a single circuit can be designed to perform both addition and subtraction of two n-bit numbers. Your answer must detail the role of the control line, the use of XOR gates, and the final carry-out bit in subtraction.
keyPoints: The control line (M) determines whether the circuit performs addition (M=0) or subtraction (M=1).
When M=1, the XOR gates invert the bits of the subtrahend (B), effectively taking the 2's complement.
The final carry-out bit acts as a borrow indicator in subtraction.
The circuit reuses the same adder hardware for both operations, simplifying the design.
